title: Screwy Pirates 2

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-04-probability-theory.md

setup.narrative: 11 pirates put their treasure in a safe. They want any MAJORITY (>= 6 of 11) to be able to open the safe, but no smaller group should be able to.
rules: Each lock can have multiple identical keys distributed among the pirates.
A group can open the safe iff they collectively hold at least one key for EVERY lock.
Find the minimum number of locks and the number of keys per pirate.
setup.question: What is the minimum number of locks? How many keys does each pirate carry?

learningObjectives: Recognize that EACH coalition of 5 pirates (no majority) must be 'blocked' by at least one lock.
Each blocking lock's keys must be held by some superset of the COMPLEMENT (6 pirates).
Count the minimum: one lock per 5-pirate subset, giving C(11, 5) = 462 locks.

stages.label: Choose your technique
stages.prompt: How do you minimize the number of locks?

options.text: For EACH 5-pirate subset (the 'blocked' coalitions), there must be one lock that they collectively cannot open. Different subsets need DIFFERENT locks (otherwise some 5-subset could open the lock). Min locks = C(11, 5) = 462.

stages.explanation: Combinatorial design problem. The minimum number of locks equals the number of distinct 5-pirate coalitions to block: C(11, 5) = 462.

stages.label: Why each 5-subset needs its own lock
stages.prompt: Why can't two different 5-pirate coalitions share the same blocking lock?

options.text: If lock L blocks coalitions A and B (both size 5), then no pirate in A holds L's key AND no pirate in B holds L's key. So the union A ∪ B (>=5 pirates) has no key for L. But A ∪ B might be a majority of >=6, who SHOULD be able to open L.
It works fine to share locks.

stages.explanation: Each lock blocks exactly one coalition of 5. The keys are held by the COMPLEMENTARY 6 pirates. Sharing a lock between coalitions A and B violates the requirement that any majority can open.

stages.label: Count locks and keys per pirate
stages.prompt: How many locks total? Each lock has C(?, ?) keys held by the complementary 6 pirates. How many of these locks does each pirate possess a key to?

options.text: Total locks = C(11, 5) = 462. Each lock has 6 keys (one per non-blocked pirate). Each pirate is in C(10, 5) = 252 distinct 6-pirate coalitions, so each pirate carries 252 keys.
Total locks = 11. Each pirate has 1 key.
Total locks = 6. Each pirate has 6 keys.

stages.explanation: Total locks: C(11, 5) = 462 (one per 5-coalition). Each lock has 6 keys (one per pirate in the complementary 6-coalition). Each pirate appears in C(10, 5) = 252 different 6-coalitions, so they carry 252 keys.

stages.label: Commit
stages.explanation: 462 locks, 252 keys per pirate. (Note: C(11, 5) = C(11, 6) = 462 by symmetry of the binomial coefficient.) The system is impractical with physical locks but illustrates the combinatorial 'threshold cryptography' concept.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize: n pirates, threshold t (majority requires >= t). Find the formulas.
For 5 pirates with majority = 3, count locks and keys per pirate.
What MODERN cryptographic primitive solves this same problem with much less physical setup?
